title: Directions & the Imperative
cefrDescriptors: Can ask for and give simple directions in a town.
Can use the imperative to give basic instructions.
concept:
Now that you can name places (Unit 7), let's get to them. This unit is about asking the way and giving directions — and it introduces a brand-new verb form: the imperative.

The imperative gives orders, instructions and directions. The trick is wonderfully simple: take the present tense and DROP the subject pronoun.
  vous tournez (you turn)  ->  Tournez ! (Turn!)
  vous continuez          ->  Continuez ! (Keep going!)
For directions you'll mostly use the vous form — it's polite and works with strangers. One small spelling rule: in the tu form of -er verbs, the imperative drops the final -s: tu tournes becomes Tourne !

The direction toolkit is tiny and high-value:
  à droite (to/on the right), à gauche (to/on the left), tout droit (straight ahead),
  tourner (to turn), continuer (to keep going), traverser (to cross), prendre (to take).
Note prendre is irregular, but its vous imperative is easy: Prenez la première rue (Take the first street).

To ask, stay polite: « Pardon, où est la gare ? » (Excuse me, where is the station?) or « Pour aller à l'hôtel, s'il vous plaît ? ». A typical answer strings the pieces together: « Continuez tout droit, puis prenez la deuxième rue à gauche. »

Practise out loud: give someone directions from your home to the nearest shop, using at least three of these verbs in the imperative.

vocabulary.exampleEn: Excuse me, where is the station?

examples.en: Excuse me, where is the station?

examples.fr: Prenez la première rue à droite.
examples.en: Take the first street on the right.

examples.fr: Traversez le pont, puis tournez à gauche.
examples.en: Cross the bridge, then turn left.
